text: Lithium metal anodes (LMAs) have attracted increasing attention as a new replacement for traditional graphite anodes due to their high capacities and excellent rate performance. To minimize the loss in capacity caused by the long cycle life and high utilization of LMAs, the lithium metal anodes generally operate at more than 95% Coulombic efficiency. As a result, the formation and thickness increase of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) are the main factors which lead to capacity loss. Unfortunately, the degradation mechanism of the SEI is still unclear. In the present work, the influence of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) solubility, which has rarely been reported, on the electrochemical performance of lithium metal anodes is investigated by exploring the composition, passivity and cycle stability in LMA-optimized ether-based electrolytes using *in operando* electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) experiments. Our results clearly show that the cycle stability is affected by the solubility of the SEI which strongly correlates with the passivity and cycle stability for different battery systems. This paper therefore explores the correlations among the solubility of SEI, electrode passivity and cycle stability. Understanding the factors of SEI stability will help to modulate the content of SEI components and influence the electrolyte environment so as to achieve the desired properties of batteries to minimize capacity loss during the cycling and long-term aging process.
explanation: Cohesive, jargon-heavy scientific writing with general claims and polished structure; while plausible as human-authored text, this style is very typical of AI-generated summaries.
